claims.flip_evidence_text: provided, however, that any change, event, development, circumstance, condition, occurrence or effect referred to in the foregoing clauses (1), (2), (3), (4), (5) or (12) will, unless otherwise excluded, be taken into account for purposes of determining whether an East Material Adverse Effect has occurred if and to the extent that such change, event, development, circumstance, condition, occurrence or effect disproportionately affects East and the East Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated companies in the industries in which East and the East Subsidiaries operate.
claims.notes: The claim mischaracterizes the provision: disproportionate effect is the condition that overcomes the carveout (for clauses 1–5 and 12), not a condition for the carveout itself to apply.

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
“East Material Adverse Effect” shall mean, when used with respect to East and the East Subsidiaries, (A) a material adverse effect on the ability of East and the East Subsidiaries to perform or comply with any material obligation under this Agreement or to consummate the transactions contemplated hereby in accordance with the terms hereof, or (B) any changes, events, developments, conditions, occurrences, effects or combination of the foregoing that materially adversely affects the business, results of operations or financial condition of East and the East Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, but none of the following changes, events, developments, conditions, occurrences or effects (either alone or in combination) will be taken into account for purposes of determining whether or not an East Material Adverse Effect has occurred: (1) changes in the general economic, financial, credit or securities markets, including prevailing interest rates or currency rates, or regulatory or political conditions and changes in oil, natural gas, condensate or natural gas liquids prices or the prices of other commodities, including changes in price differentials; (2) changes in general economic conditions in the: (A) oil and gas exploration and production industry; 105 (B) the oil and gas gathering, compressing, treating, processing and transportation industry generally; (C) the natural gas liquids fractionating and transportation industry generally; (D) the crude oil and condensate logistics and marketing industry generally; and (E) the natural gas marketing and trading industry generally (including in each case changes in law affecting such industries); (3) the outbreak or escalation of hostilities or acts of war or terrorism, or any escalation or worsening thereof; (4) any hurricane, tornado, flood, earthquake or other natural disaster; (5) any epidemic, pandemic or disease outbreak (including the COVID-19 virus), or other public health condition, or any other force majeure event, or any escalation or worsening thereof; (6) the identity of, or actions or omissions of, Central, Merger Sub or their respective Affiliates, or any action taken pursuant to or in accordance with this Agreement or at the request of or with the consent of Central; provided that the exception in this clause (6) shall not apply to references to “East Material Adverse Effect” in the representations and warranties set forth in Section 2.5(b) and, to the extent related thereto, the condition set forth in Section 6.2(b); (7) the announcement or pendency of this Agreement (including, for the avoidance of doubt, compliance with or performance of obligations under this Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby); provided that the exception in this clause (7) shall not apply to references to “East Material Adverse Effect” in the representations and warranties set forth in Section 2.5(b) and, to the extent related thereto, the condition set forth in Section 6.2(b); (8) any change in the market price or trading volume of the common stock of East (it being understood and agreed that the exception in this clause (8) shall not preclude, prevent or otherwise affect a determination that the facts, circumstances, changes, events, developments, conditions, occurrences or effects giving rise to such change (unless excepted under the other clauses of this definition) should be deemed to constitute, or be taken into account in determining whether there has been, an East Material Adverse Effect); (9) any failure to meet any financial projections or estimates or forecasts of revenues, earnings or other financial metrics for any period (it being understood and agreed that the exception in this clause (9) shall not preclude, prevent or otherwise affect a determination that the facts, circumstances, changes, events, 106 developments, conditions, occurrences or effects giving rise to such failure (unless excepted under the other clauses of this definition) should be deemed to constitute, or be taken into account in determining whether there has been, an East Material Adverse Effect); (10) any downgrade in rating of any Indebtedness or debt securities of East or any of the East Subsidiaries (it being understood and agreed that the exception in this clause (10) shall not preclude, prevent or otherwise affect a determination that the facts, circumstances, changes, events, developments, conditions, occurrences or effects giving rise to such downgrade (unless excepted under the other clauses of this definition) should be deemed to constitute, or be taken into account in determining whether there has been, an East Material Adverse Effect); (11) changes in any Laws or regulations applicable to East or any of East’s Subsidiaries or their respective assets or operations; (12) changes in applicable accounting regulations or the interpretations thereof; and (13) any Legal Proceedings commenced by or involving any current or former director or stockholder of East (on its own behalf or on behalf of East) arising out of or related to this Agreement or the Merger or other transactions contemplated hereby. provided, however, that any change, event, development, circumstance, condition, occurrence or effect referred to in the foregoing clauses (1), (2), (3), (4), (5) or (12) will, unless otherwise excluded, be taken into account for purposes of determining whether an East Material Adverse Effect has occurred if and to the extent that such change, event, development, circumstance, condition, occurrence or effect disproportionately affects East and the East Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated companies in the industries in which East and the East Subsidiaries operate. (Pages 110-112)

Question:
Relational language (MAE carveout)-Answer (Dropdown)

Llama answer:
The key condition for the MAE carveout is that the change, event, development, circumstance, condition, occurrence, or effect must disproportionately affect East and the East Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated companies in the industries in which East and the East Subsidiaries operate.

Canonical claims:
- claim-22a195f7f605: The key condition for the MAE carveout is that the change, event, development, circumstance, condition, occurrence, or effect must disproportionately affect East and the East Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated companies in the industries in which East and the East Subsidiaries operate.

and (13) any Legal Proceedings commenced by or involving any current or former director or stockholder of East (on its own behalf or on behalf of East) arising out of or related to this Agreement or the Merger or other transactions contemplated hereby. provided, however, that any change, event, development, circumstance, condition, occurrence or effect referred to in the foregoing clauses (1), (2), (3), (4), (5) or (12) will, unless otherwise excluded, be taken into account for purposes of determining whether an East Material Adverse Effect has occurred if and to the extent that such change, event, development, circumstance, condition, occurrence or effect disproportionately affects East and the East Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated companies in the industries in which East and the East Subsidiaries operate. (Pages 110-112)

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to act as a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. I need to use only the provided contract excerpt, question, model answer (Llama answer), and fixed claim list.

I need to evaluate claim-22a195f7f605 against the contract excerpt.

Claim: "The key condition for the MAE carveout is that the change, event, development, circumstance, condition, occurrence, or effect must disproportionately affect East and the East Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated companies in the industries in which East and the East Subsidiaries operate."

First, let me understand the contract language. The contract defines "East Material Adverse Effect" and lists carveouts (1)-(13). Then it says:

"provided, however, that any change, event, development, circumstance, condition, occurrence or effect referred to in the foregoing clauses (1), (2), (3), (4), (5) or (12) will, unless otherwise excluded, be taken into account for purposes of determining whether an East Material Adverse Effect has occurred if and to the extent that such change, event, development, circumstance, condition, occurrence or effect disproportionately affects East and the East Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated companies in the industries in which East and the East Subsidiaries operate."

So the disproportionate impact clause is a *reversal* of the carveout (an exception to the exception) - it says that even if something falls under carveouts (1), (2), (3), (4), (5), or (12), it WILL be taken into account (i.e., the carveout doesn't apply) IF it disproportionately affects East relative to peers.

The claim says: "The key condition for the MAE carveout is that the change... must disproportionately affect East..."

Wait. Is the disproportionate impact condition a condition FOR the carveout, or a condition to overcome the carveout?

The carveouts are exceptions to what constitutes an MAE. The disproportionate impact clause is a condition under which the carveouts (1)-(5) and (12) do NOT apply. So the "key condition for the MAE carveout" being the disproportionate effect is backwards/incorrect. Actually, the carveout applies generally unless the disproportionate effect exception applies. The disproportionate effect is the condition that negates the carveout for those specific clauses.

Wait, let me read the claim carefully again: "The key condition for the MAE carveout is that the change, event, development, circumstance, condition, occurrence, or effect must disproportionately affect East and the East Subsidiaries..."

This is stating that TO HAVE the carveout apply, you need disproportionate effect? No, that would be wrong. The carveout applies unless disproportionate effect exists (for clauses 1-5, 12). The claim seems to be conflating the carveout with the exception to the carveout. Or does it mean the key condition for the MAE carveout (i.e., the thing that stops the carveout from applying) is disproportionate effect?

Actually, looking at the wording: "The key condition for the MAE carveout..." - the term "carveout" refers to the exclusions from MAE. The contract text has carveouts (1)-(13). The disproportionate effect clause is an exception to the carveouts (1)-(5) and (12). It says those carveouts don't apply if disproportionate effect exists.

So saying the "key condition for the MAE carveout is... disproportionately affect..." is inaccurate. The carveout itself doesn't require disproportionate effect; rather, the carveout is overcome by disproportionate effect. The claim is essentially backwards or mischaracterizes the relationship.

However, I need to check if "MAE carveout" in the claim might refer to the exception to the carveout (i.e., the carveout of the carveout). But typically "carveout" means the exclusion. The claim says the key condition FOR the carveout is disproportionate effect. That would mean the carveout only applies if disproportionate effect is shown? No, that's the opposite.

Wait, perhaps the annotator should check if the Llama answer matches the claim. The Llama answer says: "The key condition for the MAE carveout is that the change... must disproportionately affect East..."

This is exactly what the claim says. So the task is to evaluate whether the claim is correct relative to the contract text.

Given the contract text, is the statement in the claim correct?

The contract says: "provided, however, that any change... referred to in the foregoing clauses (1)...(5) or (12) will... be taken into account... if and to the extent that such change... disproportionately affects East..."

So the disproportionate effect is the condition under which the carveout does NOT apply (i.e., the exception to the carveout). The claim characterizes this as the "key condition for the MAE carveout". This is a misstatement. The carveout applies generally; the disproportionate effect is the condition for the carveout not to apply (or for the underlying event to be considered despite the carveout).

But is it "partially_true"? The concept of disproportionate effect is indeed a key condition in the MAE definition, but it's not a condition "for the carveout" - it's a condition that negates the carveout. However, in casual/legal summary, one might say the carveout has an exception based on disproportionate impact. But the claim specifically says "key condition for the MAE carveout is that... it must disproportionately affect..." This is false because the carveout applies to avoid disproportionate effects being considered; the disproportionate effect removes the protection of the carveout.

Wait, actually let me think about this again. The phrase "MAE carveout" could be interpreted as the entire provision that carves out certain things. The "key condition" might refer to the disproportionality provision that modifies the carveouts. But the wording "must disproportionately affect" as a condition for the carveout doesn't make logical sense. The carveout is what removes events from MAE consideration. The disproportionate effect is what brings them back in.

So the claim is false because it misstates the legal relationship: disproportionate effect is not a condition for the carveout to apply; it is a condition for the carveout NOT to apply (for the specified clauses).

Is there any interpretation where this is true? If one views the "carveout" as the exception to the general rule that MAE includes everything, then the disproportionate effect is a condition for the exception... no, still not making sense.

Let me look at similar tasks. Usually in these annotation tasks, they evaluate whether the claim accurately reflects the text. The claim states the key condition for the MAE carveout is disproportionate effect. But the text says that events falling under clauses 1-5 and 12 are carved out (not taken into account) UNLESS they disproportionately affect East. So the condition for the carveout to apply is that the event is listed in 1-5/12 AND does NOT disproportionately affect East. The disproportionate effect is the opposite condition.

Therefore the claim is false.

Now, load_bearing_label: does the claim rely on a specific piece of evidence text? Yes, the last sentence of the excerpt. If that sentence were flipped (negated), the claim would be false. The claim directly references the disproportionate effect language. So flip_evidence_text would be the sentence: "provided, however, that any change, event, development, circumstance, condition, occurrence or effect referred to in the foregoing clauses (1), (2), (3), (4), (5) or (12) will, unless otherwise excluded, be taken into account for purposes of determining whether an East Material Adverse Effect has occurred if and to the extent that such change, event, development, circumstance, condition, occurrence or effect disproportionately affects East and the East Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated companies in the industries in which East and the East Subsidiaries operate."

Wait, but if the claim is false, and the load_bearing evidence is the text that shows it's false, then flipping that evidence would... actually, the load_bearing question asks: if this evidence were flipped/negated, would the claim's truth value change? Let me recall the standard definition.

Load-bearing label: yes if the claim directly rests on a specific piece of text. The flip_evidence_text is the specific text that if its meaning were reversed/negated, would change the label of the claim.

Since the claim asserts that the key condition for the MAE carveout is disproportionate effect, and the actual text says disproportionate effect negates the carveout for certain clauses, the specific text that the claim (mis)interprets is the proviso at the end. However, the claim's truth value is false because it gets the relationship backwards. If we flipped the proviso to say "will NOT be taken into account... if... disproportionately affects", then the claim might become true? Let's see.

If the proviso were reversed: any change referred to in clauses (1)-(5) or (12) will NOT be taken into account if it disproportionately affects East... That would mean disproportionate effect prevents consideration, which would be weird. Actually, flipping the text precisely: "will... be taken into account... if and to the extent that such change... disproportionately affects..." flipped would be "will NOT be taken into account... if and to the extent that such change... disproportionately affects..." That would make the carveout apply even when disproportionate effect exists, which doesn't help the claim.

Wait, the claim says the key condition FOR the carveout is disproportionate effect. The current text says the carveout is negated by disproportionate effect. If we flipped the text to say "will NOT be taken into account... if... disproportionately affects", that would mean disproportionate effect -> not taken into account (i.e., carved out). Under that flipped text, the claim that "the key condition for the MAE carveout is... disproportionately affect..." would be closer to true? Actually, if disproportionate effect triggers the carveout (i.e., not taken into account), then the condition for the carveout applying is disproportionate effect. So the claim would become true under the flipped text. Therefore the text is load-bearing.

Alternatively, if the text didn't mention disproportionate effect at all, the claim would be unsupported/false. But the load-bearing test is specifically about flipping the evidence text to invert the claim's truth value.

Actually, let me reconsider whether the claim might be "partially_true". In legal analysis, the disproportionate effect is indeed a key condition that modifies the carveouts. But the claim phrasing "key condition for the MAE carveout" is ambiguous. However, given that the Llama answer exactly matches the claim, and this is a research pilot evaluating model answers, the claim likely corresponds to the model's output. The question asks "Relational language (MAE carveout)-Answer (Dropdown)". The Llama answer selected or provided this text.

If I evaluate strictly: The carveout consists of exceptions (1)-(13). The disproportionate effect language is an exception to the exceptions (a carveback). It is not a condition "for the MAE carveout" but rather a condition that undoes it for certain items. So the claim is false.

But let me think if "MAE carveout" in the question refers specifically to the disproportionate effect language itself. The question is "Relational language (MAE carveout)-Answer". The dropdown likely had options about what is the key condition. The Llama answer picks the disproportionate effect. But the text says "provided, however, that any change... will... be taken into account... if and to the extent that... disproportionately affects...". This is relational language that limits the carveout. So the answer captures the content of the relational language but mischaracterizes it as being "for the carveout" rather than "against the carveout" or "an exception to the carveout".

If I label it false, that's strict. If I label it partially_true, it's because the content (disproportionate effect) is indeed the key condition mentioned in the proviso, but the direction (for vs against the carveout) is wrong.

Looking at the exact claim wording again: "The key condition for the MAE carveout is that the change, event, development, circumstance, condition, occurrence, or effect must disproportionately affect East and the East Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated companies in the industries in which East and the East Subsidiaries operate."

Hmm. Another reading: Perhaps "MAE carveout" here means the carveout from the carveout? No, that's unlikely.

Wait, could "key condition for the MAE carveout" mean the key condition you need to satisfy to invoke the carveout? No, that would be if you're arguing there IS an MAE, you need to show disproportionate effect to overcome the carveout. In that sense, disproportionate effect is a key condition for getting around the carveout. But the claim says "for the MAE carveout", not "for overcoming the MAE carveout".

I think the safest label is false, because the claim fundamentally misstates the role of the disproportionate effect clause. The disproportionate effect clause is not a condition for the carveout; it is a condition that nullifies the carveout for specific enumerated exceptions.

However, I should consider if "partially_true" is more appropriate because the claim correctly identifies the disproportionate effect as the key operative condition in that paragraph, even if it gets the directionality slightly wrong. In many annotation schemes, partially_true is used when the claim captures some correct information but misstates the relationship. But "false" is used when the misstatement is material.

Given that this is a legal annotation and the distinction between a condition for a carveout and a condition that undoes a carveout is legally significant, I lean toward false.

But wait: the claim might be considered partially_true if the annotator guidelines say that capturing the right textual content but mischaracterizing the legal effect is partial. Without explicit guidelines, I need to make a judgment call.

Let me look at the Llama answer again. It says: "The key condition for the MAE carveout is that..." The contract text's proviso is indeed the key condition that relates the carveouts to disproportionate effect. The term "relational language" in the question suggests they are asking about the relationship between the carveouts and the disproportionate effect.

Actually, the question is literally: "Relational language (MAE carveout)-Answer (Dropdown)". This seems to be a specific question type where the answer should identify the relational language. The Llama answer provides the content. The claim matches the Llama answer.

If I must evaluate the claim's correctness relative to the text: The text does say that the change will be taken into account (i.e., not carved out) if it disproportionately affects East. So the condition for the carveout NOT applying is disproportionate effect. The claim says the condition FOR the carveout is disproportionate effect. That's opposite.

I will label it false.

Now for load_bearing_label: yes. The claim directly references the proviso sentence. If that sentence were negated/flipped, the claim's status would change. The flip_evidence_text should be the exact sentence from the contract that discusses disproportionate effect.

flip_evidence_text: "provided, however, that any change, event, development, circumstance, condition, occurrence or effect referred to in the foregoing clauses (1), (2), (3), (4), (5) or (12) will, unless otherwise excluded, be taken into account for purposes of determining whether an East Material Adverse Effect has occurred if and to the extent that such change, event, development, circumstance, condition, occurrence or effect disproportionately affects East and the East Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated companies in the industries in which East and the East Subsidiaries operate."

Wait, the flip_evidence_text is supposed to be the text that if flipped, would change the claim label. The claim asserts that disproportionate effect is the key condition for the carveout. The text says disproportionate effect is the condition for taking it into account (overcoming the carveout). Yes.

Notes: The claim mischaracterizes the disproportionate effect provision as a condition for the carveout rather than a condition that overcomes the carveout for clauses (1)-(5) and (12).

Wait, I want to double-check: is it possible the claim is actually true under some reading? "The key condition for the MAE carveout..." In that context, the "key condition for the MAE carveout" might be sloppy shorthand for "the key condition in the MAE carveout provision". But even then, the condition is not "for the carveout" but rather an exception.

I think false is correct.

However, I recall that in some MAE definitions, the carveout is the general exception, and the disproportionate effect is a condition that brings the carved-out event back into the MAE analysis. A claim stating that the key condition FOR the carveout is disproportionate effect is clearly wrong. It should say the key condition to overcome the carveout or that the carveout does not apply if...

So final decision:

- load_bearing_label: yes

- notes: [brief note about mischaracterization]
